items.q: What happens during coagulation when an egg is cooked?
items.correct: The proteins set, changing the egg from liquid to solid.
items.misconception: The egg absorbs water and swells like starch.
items.random_wrong: The egg releases sugar and browns.
items.why: Students confuse protein coagulation with starch gelatinisation or sugar caramelisation.

items.q: What causes a starch-based sauce to thicken when heated?
items.correct: Gelatinisation, as starch grains absorb liquid and swell.
items.random_wrong: The protein in the flour setting solid.
items.why: Learners attribute thickening to evaporation or protein rather than starch gelatinisation.

items.q: What is the purpose of 'dovetailing' when cooking a meal?
items.correct: Organising tasks so several dishes are ready at the same time.
items.misconception: A way of decorating the edges of pastry.
items.why: Students misread the term as a presentation or storage technique rather than time planning.

items.q: Egg white turning from runny to solid when heated is an example of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Coagulation. “Gelatinisation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Starch grains swelling and thickening a sauce when heated is called:
items.why: Correct answer: Gelatinisation. “Coagulation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Planning the order of tasks so dishes finish together is known as:
items.why: Correct answer: Dovetailing. “Garnishing” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
